content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAI R POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
·, 1102 Q St reet• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
'I (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
. 1/ 
-
, 
Randi Wallach 
Assistant Counsel 
April 17, 2018 
Bay Area Air Quality Management District 
375 Beale Street, Suite 600 
San Francisco, CA 9410  
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-283 
Dear Ms. Wallach: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Bay Area Air Quality 
Management District (the District), a regional air pollution control agency, regarding the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the Act). 1 Please note that we are only providing 
advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of 
interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Note also that we are 
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice 
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
Is the 375 Beale Condominium Corporation (the Corporation), fonned to manage the affair¡ 
of the building where Bay Area regional government agencies are housed, along with other non­
governmental tenants, required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code under the PRA? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Corporation is a local government agency under the Act and is required to adopt a 
conflict of interest code for its members or be included within an existing code. 
FACTS 
You are an authorized District representative. The District's headquarters are in a new 
building at 375 Beale Street in San Francisco (the building), along with several other Bay Area 
government agencies, including the Metropolitan Transportation Commission (MTC), Bay Area 
ToH Authority (the Toll Authority), and the Association of Bay Area Governments (the 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-283 
Page No. 2 
Association), a joint powers authority established by the San Francisco Bay Area•s cities, towns and 
counties pursuant to the California Joint Exercise of Powers Act. The building also houses non­
governmental tenants, including commercial office and retail space. 
The Corporation is a nonprofit mutual benefit corporation that was formed to: 
(l) 
provide for the management, maintenance and care of certain common areas and 
jointly used space in the building; 
(2) 
assist the owners of the condo units in operating their units and shared facilities; 
(3) 
undertake other functions and activities ancillary to the operation of the condo units 
for governmental purposes; and 
(4) 
engage in any ancillary or related activities, to reduce the burdens of government for 
each of the unit owners. 
The property is divided into four condominium units. The District and the Association each 
own one unit. The Bay Area Headquarters Authority, a joint powers authority established by MTC 
and the Toll Authority pursuant to the California Joint Exercise of Powers Act, owns one unit. 
The Corporation is funded by the unit owners, all of which are currently government 
entities. If a unit owner sold its unit, then the other unit owners would have a right of first refusal. If 
they pass on that right, then the seller has pledged to give preference to other government entities 
that may wish to purchase the unit. It is possible that a unit could be bought by a non-governmental 
entity in the future. 
The Corporation's principal purpose is to manage the shared building space of the 
government entity unit owners and non-government entity tenants. Government entities are legally 
authorized to and have traditionally purchased and managed property for their operations. Private 
parties undertake these obligations as well. 
ANALYSIS 
A. 
Conflict of Interest Requirements. 
The Act prohibits a public official from making or participating in making a governmental 
decision in which the official knows or has reason to know he or she has a financial interest. 
(Section 87100.) The conflict of interest provisions of the Act apply only to "public officials." The 
Act defines a "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local 
government agency. (Section 82048.) 
In addition, Section 87300 of the Act provides that "[e]very agency shall adopt and 
promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code" that applies to its "designated employees." The Act defines 
"agency" as any state or local government agency. (Section 82003.) A "local government agency" 
is "a county, city, or district of any kind including school district, or any other local or regional 
political subdivision, or any department, division, bureau, office, board, commission, or any other 
agency of the foregoing." (Section 82041.) 


B. 
Applying the Siegel Test. 
File No. A-17-283 
Page No. 3 
The Commission uses a four-part factual test to distinguish governmental from non­
governmental entities. (In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62.) In the Siegel opinion, the issue was 
whether the Pico Rivera Water Development Corporation (Pico), a nonprofit corporation, should be 
considered a local government entity. Pico was founded to acquire, maintain, and operate a water 
system. In analyzing whether Pico was a governmental entity, the Commission set forth the four 
criteria listed below, which are applied to the present case. 
1. Did the impetus for formation o(the entity originate with a government entity? 
Generally, the first criterion of the Siegel test is met where an entity is created by statute or 
ordinance or by some official action of another governmental agency. (Maas Advice Letter, No. A-
98-261.) In this case, the government agencies housed in the building were the impetus for creating, 
and each took action to jointly form, the Corporation. 
2. Is the entity substantially funded by. or is its primary source offimds, a government agency? 
The Corporation is funded by the unit owners, all of which are government entities. Thus, 
the second criterion of the Siegel test is met. 
3. Is one of the principal purposes for which the entity is formed to provide services or undertake 
obligations that public agencies are legally authorized to perform and which, in fact, they 
traditionallv have performed? 
In the Siegel Opinion, the third criterion is a two-part inquiry that examines whether an 
entity performs a public function, and whether the service provided is one that is traditionally 
performed by public agencies. (Stark Advice Letter, No. A-03-015.) 
When considering whether an entity serves a public function one factor is the degree to 
which government actors control or are involved in the entity's operations. The Corporation's 
principal purpose is to manage the shared building space of the government entity unit owners and 
non-government entity tenants. 
Another factor is whether the service is one that is traditionally performed by public 
agencies. Government entities are legally authorized to purchase and manage property for their 
operations, and have traditionally done so. Private parties undertake these obligations as well. 
4. Is the entity treated as a public entity by other statutory provisions? 
According to the Corporation's Articles of Incorporation, board meetings are subject to the 
Brown Act. Thus, the Corporation is treated as a public entity. 
It is not necessary that all four of the criteria be satisfied for an entity to be considered a 
government agency. (0 'Shea Advice Letter, No. A-91-570.) It is only necessary that the entity 
satisfy enough of the four criteria for its overall character to correspond to that of a local 
government agency. (Rasiah Advice Letter, No. A-01-020.) 


File No. A-17-283 
Page No. 4 
We find that, based on the facts presented, all four factors of the Siegel test are met. Thus, 
we conclude that the Corporation is a local public agency within the meaning of the Act, it is 
required to have a conflict of interest code for its members or be included within a code pursuant to 
Section 87300, and its members are subject to regulation under the Act. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JMF,Jr. :jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
AssistruitGeneral Counsel 
 
 
-;;---:--­
. Feser Jr.
